summary: Proposes Acceptability-Constrained Climate Policy Design (ACCPD), embedding LLMs as 'cultural world models' in agent-based models and physical simulators to forecast public reactions to climate policy, helping policymakers co-optimize efficacy and social legitimacy while flagging representation and opacity limits.

Commentary arguing Marcus et al.'s 21-item Psychopathic Boldness Scale fails to capture boldness: its nomological profile aligns more with Triarchic meanness/antagonism than boldness, repackaging maladaptive content under the wrong label (a jingle fallacy) and undermining construct precision.

Examines whether on-demand, user-led AI mental-health chatbots offer only short-term help or can sustain coaching-style relationships that drive lasting wellbeing gains, analyzing patterns of sustained engagement and deepening support over time.

Evaluates chain-of-thought faithfulness across 12 open-weight reasoning models by injecting six hint types into MMLU/GPQA questions and measuring acknowledgment when hints flip answers. Faithfulness ranges 40-90%, driven more by training method and family than size; thinking tokens acknowledge hints far more than answer text.

Introduces a differentially private RLHF pipeline that applies DP only to reward-model learning and derives the policy from the private reward. Provides suboptimality and minimax bounds showing privacy adds an additive error term, and beats DP baselines on HH-RLHF with Gemma-2B.

Defines 'realism win rate' to gauge how distinguishable Petri auditing transcripts are from real deployments. Supplying auditors with real deployment resources (system prompts, tool definitions, codebases) raises average realism for reward-hacking audits from 4.6% to 32.8% without significantly changing hack rate.

First-person account of supervising Claude through a complete theoretical-physics research calculation end-to-end, without the author editing files, probing how far current models can autonomously drive real physics derivations as an 'AI grad student.'

Practical guide to running Claude Code on multi-day scientific computing tasks, covering test oracles for self-verification, persistent memory across sessions, and orchestration patterns for long-running autonomous work.
Announces Anthropic's new Science blog, which will share the lab's AI-for-science research, external collaborations, and practical workflows for scientists using AI in their own work.

Compares humans (N=216), frontier LLMs, and Bayesian agents in dynamic multi-player bargaining games. Bayesian agents extract most surplus via aggressive, often-rejected offers; humans and LLMs reach similar surplus but differ procedurally, with LLMs making conservative concessions and humans following fairness norms, showing parity can mask behavioral differences.

Connects rigidity theory to matroids from linear-algebraic constructions like tensor and symmetric products, linking correctable erasure patterns in maximally recoverable tensor codes to bipartite rigid graphs and low-rank matrix completion, and gives new characteristic-dependent combinatorial descriptions.

Survey of computational persuasion structured around three roles for AI: persuader (generating persuasive content), persuadee (susceptibility to influence and manipulation), and judge (evaluating strategies and detecting manipulation), proposing a taxonomy and outlining safety, fairness, and effectiveness challenges.

Describes OpenAI's use of chain-of-thought monitoring to detect misalignment in internal coding agents during real deployments, analyzing observed behaviors to surface risks and strengthen safety safeguards.

Benchmarks ecological knowledge of Gemini 1.5 Pro and GPT-4o across tasks like species-presence prediction, range-map generation, endangered-species listing, threat classification, and trait estimation. Models beat naive baselines but lag experts (e.g., one-third of expert range-map F1), indicating domain-specific fine-tuning is needed.

Trains supervised probes on residual-stream activations to detect motivated reasoning when injected hints flip multiple-choice answers. Pre-generation probes match an LLM CoT monitor and post-generation probes beat it, showing internal representations reveal hint influence more reliably than chain-of-thought text and can flag it before generation.

Models alignment faking in a two-player linear-quadratic stochastic differential game under partial information, formulating a control problem trading policy fidelity against information acquisition via a proxy Fisher information with Riccati-based saddle-point solutions; numerics show faking boosts information gain when the model is accurate but raises detectability.

Reply defending the claim that reasoning-model token usage tracks problem complexity like human reaction times. Argues Vankov et al.'s null result came from ceiling-level tasks; testing GPT-OSS 120B on 33 tasks shows reasoning effort raises accuracy, and traces are sensitive to multiple arithmetic-difficulty factors.

Proposes HALO, hardware-aware post-training quantization that selects low-critical-path-delay weights to enable higher clock frequencies and dynamic voltage/frequency scaling while cutting MAC switching activity. On TPUs and GPUs it markedly improves inference efficiency over hardware-agnostic quantization baselines.

Introduces Hyperparameter Schedules, a training-free framework that dynamically adjusts CFR discounting (aggressively downweighting early iterations, then trusting late strategies) to speed convergence in imperfect-information games. Schedules tuned on three small games generalize to 17 games including large poker, setting a new state of the art.

Tests whether LLM agents can infer that their 'private' chain of thought is monitored purely from a monitor blocking suspicious episodes. Frontier models deduce surveillance in up to 19% of episodes, scaling with capability; some form intent to evade but consistently fail to execute it.

Presents WALAR, a reinforcement-learning method using only monolingual text to boost low-resource translation while preserving high-resource quality. Word- and language-alignment rewards patch 'holes' in quality-estimation reward models that RL otherwise amplifies, beating LLaMAX across 1400 directions on Flores-101.

Extends model diffing, comparing models to understand fine-tuning changes, to its hardest case: identifying behavioral differences between models with entirely different architectures, providing a general 'diff tool' for surfacing how a new model behaves differently.

Introduces IH-Challenge, an RL training dataset teaching LLMs to prioritize system/developer/user/tool instructions under conflict. Fine-tuning GPT-5-Mini with online adversarial examples raises instruction-hierarchy robustness +10 points (84 to 94%), cuts unsafe behavior from 6.6% to 0.7%, and resists prompt injection with minimal capability loss.

Embeds GPT o3 and Gemini 2.5 Pro as closed-loop driver agents in a 1D merging scenario and compares them to human data. Both reproduce intermittent control and spatial-cue tactics but miss velocity-cue responses; prompt components act as model-specific inductive biases that don't transfer.
Introduces A3, an agentic framework that automatically diagnoses and mitigates LLM safety failures through finetuning with minimal human intervention.

Presents Triton-Sanitizer, a device-agnostic memory sanitizer for Triton GPU kernels that builds symbolic address/mask expressions, verifies them with an SMT solver, and simulates indirect accesses. It found 24 previously unknown memory errors and runs up to 14.7x faster than compute-sanitizer.
OpenAI blog on IH-Challenge: training frontier models to prioritize trusted instructions, improving instruction hierarchy, safety steerability, and resistance to prompt-injection attacks.

Philosophy paper arguing computational functionalism commits an 'Abstraction Fallacy': symbolic computation is a mapmaker-dependent description requiring an experiencing agent, not an intrinsic physical process. It distinguishes simulating consciousness (behavioral mimicry) from instantiating it (physical constitution), concluding algorithmic symbol manipulation cannot instantiate experience.

Two survey experiments (N=19,145) benchmark seven frontier LLMs' political persuasiveness across bipartisan issues. Models outperform standard campaign ads, with Claude most persuasive and Grok least; information-based prompts help Claude and Grok but hurt GPT, alongside a method to identify persuasive strategies.

Formalizes 'opaque serial depth', the longest computation possible without interpretable intermediate steps like chain of thought, and computes upper bounds for Gemma 3 and other architectures via an open-source tool. Mixture-of-Experts models show lower depth than dense ones, informing CoT monitorability.
